title: Prof. David Silver | The Era of Experience - YouTube
text:
David Sid from Google and so David will
share with us the area of experience.
Thank you.
>> Thank you very much. Uh so it's a
pleasure to be here. Um it's a great
topic for a workshop and um happy to
tell you about um maybe a little bit the
bigger picture of um how I see the
future of AI unrolling as we move
forwards into um into the next years. Um
and then I'm going to talk specifically
about some of the recent work that
connects to that. Uh including uh it's
very timely to be giving this talk
because actually there's uh two nature
papers that I'm talking about in this
paper. One of them just uh appeared a
couple of weeks ago and one of them just
came out yesterday. So it's uh it's very
timely to be able to to talk about them.
Um although the work itself um is is not
completely new.
Okay.
So I want to start by talking about
where we are in this current era of AI.
So I think it's fair to say that most of
the work that has been done in AI
recently sits squarely in what I call
the era of human data. And so what I
mean by this is that if you think about
how that AI has been built, how it
thinks, how it acts, you know, the whole
design of the system is uh based around
human data and human interaction.
So first of all, the AI learns primarily
from human data trained from the
internet. So this is like all the
natural language that can be found
anywhere. It's all distilled into this
giant model and then it's fine-tuned
from human feedback specifically you
know humans give their examples of good
behavior and they give their preferences
to help fine-tune these systems and this
has led to a lot of progress. Um so we
have LLMs now with wide ranging
knowledge. I'm sure you all use them um
and you know maybe many times per day
and we have amazing performance in
everything from you know law to math
through to software
but you know if we keep our eyes on the
on the grand prize of AI uh this kind of
AI is not going to take us all the way
it's not going to take us in isolation
it can't deliver super intelligence and
you know this is the goal which um at
least I have been working towards for my
my career Okay. Um, and so why can't it
do this? I think fundamentally it's
because a system that's trained solely
from human knowledge doesn't have the
ability to discover new knowledge or new
paradigms that aren't already in the
human data.
So in a sense, it hasn't really answered
the really big question, the deepest
scientific question of the whole of AI,
which is how can an agent learn for
itself?
For me, I hope that's why you're all
sitting here because I think you know
the topics which people are addressing
here. Uh you reinforcement learning,
stocastic control. These are methods
which are are scientifically trying to
access this question of how can a system
learn for itself. And that's really
important. We shouldn't let go of that
that key concept.
So to give some contrast to the era of
human data, we should think about what
it's like to do the opposite. what
what's it like to learn from experience?
And so I like to show this this video.
Some of you may have seen it before, but
it's a it's, you know, a time-lapse
video of this u one baby with which has
a lot of stamina, I have to say, for for
playing. Um, and it plays and plays and
plays in its environment. Um, and what's
it doing? Well, if you look at it from
time to time, it becomes fascinated by
the objects in its environment. It
spends a, you know, sets itself
seemingly these sub goals of, oh, I'm
going to play with this object. Oh,
look. I found this thing on the wall
that kind of, you know, boings backwards
and forwards. I don't even know what it
is, but I'm going to play with it and
try and figure out its behavior. Then it
moves on to the next thing and tries to
understand it. And it's constantly
challenging itself. It's got this
curiosity and this ability to adapt and
learn new skills u which goes on for in
this case, you know, multiple hours. And
if you think about uh human life, this
process goes on for many years. And the
skills which are required in this way
ultimately allow uh the mind of this
baby to be uh to have skills which
enable it to do amazing things later in
its life. It can become a neurosurgeon.
It can become a dancer, a craftsman, a
pianist, a scientist or a tennis player
to name you know just a very small
handful of the possibilities.
And so we should be asking ourselves,
how can we build systems that have this
amazing capacity to learn and learn and
learn and keep discovering new skills
and new knowledge at Infinitum.
So um this takes us towards what I call
uh the era of experience and I see this
as just an inevitable transition that at
some point for AI to progress far beyond
where we are now, it's necessary for
this transition to take place. the
agents will start to learn from their
own experience. Um, and what I mean by
experience is um, an agent that
interacts with its own environment and
learns from those interactions.
So this this is a kind of necessary
prerequisite to make further progress in
AI. Um and this will be done I think one
day at vast scales maybe so large that
it makes the internet seem tiny and that
you know the scale of human data of uh
you know that LLMs have been trained on
will one day seem you know just a very
small thing. Um and I think when this
happens it will be transformative for AI
um because once we have the continual
ability to discover new knowledge and
capabilities you know like that human
baby but maybe even far beyond one day
um that this will you know potentially
lead to incredible forms of of
intelligence and capabilities.
So, you know, we can think about what
are some of the characteristics that
that um this era of experience will
have. Um, and I think just a few of them
would be to say that like that baby in
the video, we should expect our future
agents in the era of experience to
inhabit like long streams of experience
to have lifetimes to, you know, just
have the ability to keep learning and
learning and learning in whatever stream
of experience they're in. And these
dreams will be long and complex and it
will be able to pick up more and more
skills and knowledge.
The actions and observations of these
agents will be, you know, part of the
environment. They'll be grounded in the
environment. They won't, you know, it
won't be some human like dialogue with
an LLM. uh it'll be something where the
agent's actually able to do something in
the environment and receive feedback
based on what the consequence of its
actions actually was upon its
environment not a human saying you know
um I I I predict that that you know I I
value that action or this action and the
rewards will be grounded in experience
as well so you know the feedback it gets
won't just be observations but really
you know a sense of something works in
in in its environment or something
doesn't work and finally these agents
will plan um or reason about the
experience itself. I think these four
characteristics are all very different
from where today's AI systems are. LLMs
aren't doing any of these things. And so
there's huge opportunity to progress
beyond where we are once we kind of
embrace this inevitable transition and
start to spend our time exploring it.
So then you might ask, well, you know,
weren't we here already a few years ago?
you know we uh built systems in the past
um things like you know Atari DQN and
Alph Go and Alphazero you know these are
some things that we did and there's many
other great breakthroughs that happened
using uh reinforcement learning in the
past
um I think you know those were immensely
successful and and led to a kind of
surge of interest in in kind of methods
based on experience so on the y- axis
here is like you know the the attention
of the AI community on reinforcement
learning you know, so you shouldn't
think of this as, you know, necessarily
that that that um um LLMs are bad and
that that this is good. It's just
saying, you know, there was a lot of
attention at that time um and exciting
things happened, but at that time they
were quite restricted in general to um
narrow domains that were based in
simulation.
So then you know GPT3 and chat GPT and
things like this came along and people
became very excited because these could
seemingly do things that you know
engaged with real world knowledge and
and and people could interact with them
and ask about things which weren't just
part of some narrow simulation. So that
seemed very exciting. Uh but at the same
time I think it's fair to say that we
ended up throwing the baby out with the
bathwater that these systems no longer
have the ability to discover knowledge
for themselves from experience. And so I
think you know what remains is the right
hand side of this figure which is I
think there'll be a renewed inevitably a
renewed interest in methods based on
experience um and we're starting to see
some examples of that some which I'll
talk about um where uh methods based
experience are tackling you know complex
open-ended challenge domains um and
complex you know interactive uses in the
in at least the digital um part of the
real world and I think you know as that
gets embraced it will need uh with to
you potentially unbounded performance.
So okay the experience so far has been
under this lamp post on the left you
know it's been focused on the era of
human data and so far a very small
amount of experience has been focused on
the thing which has the greatest
potential and so I guess you know if you
take nothing else away from this I'd
encourage people to start looking under
that second lampost and I think this is
the kind of group of people that really
understand what that lampost looks like
and and you know we really need to be
embracing that lampost and seeing you
know what can be done there.
So
another way to talk about this is to say
you know in this terms of what happened
I think
you know human data provides the
shortcut in some sense. So we we we
didn't solve the deep problem of AI we
solved you know sort of a shallow
problem of AI this idea of distilling
the knowledge of human data into the
machine. And and one way to think of
that, a nice analogy that I I I like to
borrowed is this idea that, you know, we
found fossil fuels. Like the internet's
like the fossil fuel of of of AI. You
know, there was just all this amazing
stuff that was just already there in the
ground and AI was able to tap into those
fossil fuels and make use of it. Um and
so, you know, these LLMs have basically,
you know, been earning the fossil fuels
um to pre-train the these these amazing
agents. And that's been really really
cheap. you know, it's far cheaper than
actually going out and interacting the
world. You can gather all of this
experience for free. Um, but the problem
with fossil fuels is that they run out.
And I think that's what's happened with
uh with the era of human data that you
know most of the good data um has been
consumed. It's been trained upon. We
have systems that have like literally
trained on you know every paper that's
ever been written etc. Um so now to go
further we have to solve this deep
problem of AI. uh we need to find the
renewable energy the thing which will
allow us to keep learning forever and
that renewable energy is experience. If
you have experience and you keep
learning from our own interactions um
that there's yeah you know there's no
end to that you can just keep keep going
and going and going and systems one day
will be able to just keep interacting
with their environments and learning
more and more and more. So in that sense
it's renewable. Um, and then you can ask
well well where does this generality
come from that was so helpful with with
things like chat GPT and so forth where
they had this very general way to talk
about almost anything and I think the
answer there is that the world provides
the generality that the world contains
just this vast array of signals within
it you know there's just so many
different things that we can all do and
interact with and and and the world has
vast potential for generality um so you
know if we just build systems that can
optimize any and all of those signals u
these these these systems will be
extremely general but you then have to
pay the cost of interaction and I think
you know that's that's the point here
that that there's in the end the
shortcuts are going to run out um and we
have to pay the cost of experience you
have to build systems that actually
interact with the world and if we don't
do that, you know, it's not like there's
some magic button we can press that
allows systems to magically understand
how to discover new knowledge. It's just
it's not going to happen until we pay
the cost of building systems that
actually interact with our environment
and then we'll be able to solve the deep
problem of AI.
Okay. So I wanted to talk now about some
steps um towards uh the era of
experience. So you know some of these
are success stories everyone's very
familiar with. You know we've seen
systems that have played board games you
know really really well or achieved um
superhuman capability in board games.
We've seen systems that have been able
to, you know, interact, you know,
robotic systems that have solved um, you
know, really nice dextrous manipulation
problems. Um, we've seen AI play, uh,
video games. Um, and, uh, and we've seen
AI, you know, deal with things like
mathematics that I'll be talking about
shortly.
So I think the point I want to make here
is that when people have pushed on
learning from experience it has
succeeded you know in these cases like
people when people have pushed hard with
determination conviction that it's going
to work and put a lot of resource into
these things incredible things have
happened you know we we've literally
built systems that have become super
intelligent in these limited spheres um
so I think really what's lacking at the
moment is just that that kind of
conviction that conviction iction has
gone. People are looking under the under
the lamppost. So these success stories
should if nothing else remind us that
given conviction that um you know
success is possible.
So I'm going to talk about three case
studies now which I've been you know
lucky enough to work on. U the first of
which you know happened a long time ago
but I'm just going to briefly remind
people on it because the others um are
going to build on this. Um and that was
the alpha zero um algorithm. Um, and I
have to say when I look back at my
career, uh, I think it's it's the thing
I'm most proud of. And I'm most proud of
it because, uh, because it felt magical,
you know, had this magic of learning
from experience that you could just have
something that it started from nothing.
And over time, this system that started
with no human knowledge whatsoever um
learned all the skills and capabilities
of really complex um human domains, you
know, domains where humans had spent
their entire careers like learning all
the knowledge there is to learn. And
these systems have just learned for
themselves to um you know basically
acquire and then exceed all of the
knowledge that humankind had ever
acquired in these domains. I think
there's something very beautiful about
that. And the algorithm was incredibly
simple. It was just these three steps
and literally nothing else.
So step one of the algorithm was to do
planning. Um so this step one was
achieved using Monte Carlo research and
the Monte Carlo research was guided by
the current policy and value function.
Step two was to do a policy improvement
step. So to update the policy towards
the best action that was found by the
Monte Carlo research. So that was step
two. And step three was a policy
evaluation step. So to update the value
function towards the outcome of games
that were played using um this Monte
Carlo research to do planning.
Um so that's the entire algorithm. Um,
and when you instantiate this algorithm,
you get these incredible results where
actually in just a few hours, it was
able to go from um completely random
neural network with no knowledge at all
the way through to superhuman
performance in in all the games we
applied it to. Um, so chess, shogi, and
go. Um so I think it took something like
uh um four hours to beat the um the
existing computer champion in chess
which it was itself already superhuman
um and also um achieved the the world's
best performance in these other games.
Um so you know this is an a very pure
example of the power of learning from
experience. Uh just a little anecdote
would be that in fact this was so
experiential that the game of Shogi, you
know, us the the authors of this system,
none of us even knew how to play Shogi.
Um and so we we we put this system
together. Um we, you know, we ran the
system literally the first time we'd
ever run it, first training training
run. Um we we ran it on on Shogi and we
didn't even know if it was good. Like at
the end of this, we weren't sure. Um, so
we we showed it to uh to you the Dennis,
the CEO of DeepMind. He's a great games
player and he's like, "Oh, this looks
really good. I'm gonna show this to the
world champion." And the world champion
came back and said it was like the most
incredible games of Shogi you'd ever
seen. So, you know, it's some something
about this process is is very uh elegant
and and has the potential to do things
that we don't know. You know, it can it
can learn skills that we don't put into
the system.
Okay. moving into the present day. Um so
last year we built a system called alpha
proof uh which was an application of um
reinforcement learning and using this
alpha zero algorithm to mathematics. Uh
and so the basic idea here was to treat
the whole of formal mathematics as a
kind of game and then apply exactly this
same method that we just saw the alpha
zero method to apply it um to the game
of mathematics. Um and so you in this
case it's like a a single player game.
Um and
so formal mathematics for those who
don't know um there there are these
beautiful systems. we used a a a
language called lean. Um and in lean um
all of mathematics that can in principle
be expressed uh it's possible to express
in lean. So it's you know very general
and capable language and human
mathematicians have expressed you know
large amounts of human mathematics in in
this language of lean. Um and what
happens is basically um the way this
progresses let me just use the try and
use the pointer as this progresses
basically the agent um
can create like a new tactic in lean. Um
so you kind of have a theorem that
you're trying to solve which has been
expressed as like this kind of
hypothesis that you're trying to prove.
Um and then what happens is the agent
can basically receives this description
of the kind of state it's in um which is
just a description at this point of the
starting state and then it can take
actions uh which are lean tactics and
lean tactics are basically way to
manipulate the state of the solver. uh
for example you know this one here
introduces like a local variable into
the into its state and there are many
other tactics that can be taken like um
you know proof by contradiction or
induction or all these different things.
So everything that you're familiar with
in ma mathematics, all of these steps
can be taken um and then you can build
essentially a program um to prove
anything um any theorem in mathematics.
And the beautiful thing about formal
mathematics unlike LLMs is that you know
for sure if you've proved something.
It's like you you know it's a it's a
perfect proof like it's you're just
done. like if you have a if you if you
get to the kind of end state where
you've proved something um you know that
your proof is is is perfect. Um so then
we apply alpha zero to this game.
Basically um we built a neural network
uh which takes as an input this uh state
of the system this tactic state um which
estimates the the value function and a
policy just like in we did for the game
of go and so forth. Um and the policy uh
outputs tactics
um so yeah just like everything else
we've done before and then we can do a
search in terms of those tactics and try
and find a sequence of tactics that
actually solves u the the theorem that
proves the theorem.
Okay. So
um so this you can now look at this is a
paper that came out yesterday. We we
announced results last year but the the
the nature paper finally came out. Um so
that's um you can now see these uh these
plots um which are things like this.
There's there's much more detail in the
paper if you're interested in uh but
what you see is that when you start with
this system um in this case it's it's
not starting from a like a completely
vanilla neural network. It starts from a
very simple small LLM um that's
basically um been trained um on on um
you know things like archive. Uh it's
very very simple. So it basically knows
almost nothing about mathematics at the
beginning and then over time um it gets
better and better and better um until um
at this point here it solves um you know
97% of the standard kind of benchmark
for formal mathematics that previously
uh no one had done very well on before.
Um, and then here we see how well it's
doing on on actual problems from the
International Mathematics Olympiad,
which are for those who don't know
considered to be like these super
complex challenging problems that are
worked on each year by kind of the young
mathematical geniuses um from all over
the world who come together and train in
kind of boot camps to um become you know
incredible mathematicians. Um so you
know we we solved about 40% of all of
the historical problems um here and and
these green ones this is how we did on
Putnham which is a similar um the
similar kind of uh test for um
university level mathematics.
Um so that was the kind of alpha zero
applied to learning from experience. But
then we did something else on top of
that which is we said okay well what if
we wanted to now plan from experience?
What if we wanted to start from that
base level of performance and run the
same algorithm again like run so this
this thing here was learning about like
um millions of problems that we'd kind
of extracted from all over the place.
We'd kind of taken every single human
problem we could find and we'd
formalized it in some way into into lean
and we applied the system to uh all of
those
uh
and so so here we're basically training
on a vast number of different problems
and as a held out consequence you know
we our held out performance on IMO is
shown here over here what we do is we
apply exactly the same algorithm but to
those specific IMO problems that we we
care about so here is like you start
from a particular IMO problem and the
system basically just tries to figure
out how can I solve that problem and so
it's trying to solve loads of different
variants of that of that specific
problem. And we see that it starts off
at this performance that we had here,
39% um and it rapidly through applying
alpha zero kind of on the fly. You know,
this is something it does in a few hours
and it gets better and better and better
by applying this thing on the fly it
learns to get much better performance
there. So you know really really doing
very well and it should be said it also
gets to 100% on this kind of um standard
uh benchmark that people have been
measuring. So it's nice to have cleaned
that one out.
Okay. So when we applied this, this is
exactly the system that we applied last
year um in the international mathematics
olympiad. Um and it was applied in two
completely unseen problems that had been
devised by the mathematical community to
be you know super challenging and
unknown. Um and we took slightly longer
because of this approach of planning
from experience. It took slightly longer
than humans. So humans get nine hours to
solve these problems. Uh we took uh
three days to solve this problem. But we
we um got 28 points which was just one
point short of the gold medal threshold.
And this is the first time that anyone
has ever achieved a medal in the
international ma mathematics olympiad
using AI. So this was a real it's
considered one of the grand challenges
of AI for for some years to to to reach
this level of performance.
Um so there was one problem in
particular. So this is uh uh problem six
the hardest problem from that year's
IMO. Um and this problem uh was so hard
that u less than 2% of those young
mathematical geniuses were able to solve
this problem. Um and alpha proof solved
it perfectly. Um and this is actually
the proof that it found. Um so you can
see that it's generating quite complex
like um programs in in lean. Um, and
yeah, I I I wouldn't expect anyone here
to, you know, it would take a while to
unpick all of this, but I think the
interesting thing is just to say, you
know, it it created something very
complex to be able to find a perfect
solution to this this problem that that
very few human mathematicians had ever
been able to solve.
Um,
okay. Fine. Finally,
>> it's solving this hard problem, why do
you think it only solves 60% of them?
>> Yeah, it's a good question. So, the
question was um if it's solving the
hardest problem, why is it only solving
60% of all problems? There's two answers
to this. So, first of all, the plot that
I showed you uh was using very limited
compute. You know, this was using like
we were this this compute was being like
measured across all historical
IMO problems. So the compute was being
shared amongst all historical IMO
problems. When we ran on the IMO 2024,
we concentrated the same amount of
compute just on the six problems that we
were trying to solve for that year. So
there's more compute was used here. It's
the first answer. And the second answer
is that um um alpha proof is very strong
at certain types of mathematics and very
weak at others. Um so you know this kind
of problem in in um in algebra it's it's
like great at um it's much weaker at
compliatorics problems which uh often
are very hard to express even in lean.
Um so there was a problem that we didn't
solve in this year's in that in this
2024 IMO um which a lot of humans also
hated but it was like basically
required
pages and pages of lean just to
formalize the problem.
>> Um and then uh the solution was kind of
like a particular very intuitive idea
that humans kind of needed to pull out
of sort seemingly by magic. those kind
of things. I think we're still some way
away from solving with these kinds of
methods.
>> Um yes and no. So um so we do have an
automated method. Um and in fact um the
automated method um um basically on all
of the problems that we solved we
correctly um formalized the problem. Um
but for for example that very difficult
combinatorics problem we didn't
correctly autoformmalize it and so when
we actually ran the competition we used
human formalized um examples in
principle the system can formalize these
things for itself and is is actually
pretty good at that for IMO type
problems. Yeah.
>> Uh we have built something like that
internally but that's not yet available
externally. I should say that there is
now a public tool that came out
alongside um the the nature paper um
which allows people to play with alpha
proof and and hopefully accelerate the
the world's mathematics by enabling
people to to you know um solve any any
theorem or conjecture that you you care
about. Um so it's yes it's very good for
certain um certain use cases.
It's not yet like the real deal. It's
not like you know solving the reman
hypothesis or something. It's far off um
that kind of mathematics.
Okay. So I want to talk finally about a
third case study. Um so this is another
um piece of work. We actually did this a
couple of years ago but um it was um
only published um couple two or three
weeks ago. Um and it's really I think
another very beautiful example of of
learning from experience. I think um uh
it in a in a different way to alpha zero
but it basically is something which has
really learned something I think quite
profound about the nature of learning
from experience
and I think the problem here is trying
to address is the fact that you know RL
itself reinforcement learning is hard to
to do well um and some of the reasons
it's hard I'm sure you're all familiar
with these but you know it has a
non-ifferiable objective the best recipe
varies between environments and there's
no general purpose like robust algorithm
that just works across any environment
you you might drop it into. You know,
we're lacking those things. In some
sense, you could say, you know, the
level of maturity for learning from
experience isn't yet at the level that
it things just work.
So, we tried to address this problem uh
and and the way we had tried to address
it was actually by learning the
algorithm itself from experience. Like,
you know, what if the system itself
could learn the right way to to do RL
Um and so what we did was we basically
uh built a system uh where we had
many many different simulated
environments you know things like Atari
environments and so forth and we had you
know like a hundred different
environments and we had allowed the
agent to interact in each of those
environments using like a a learning
rule. So if you think of the learning
rule of like Q-learning or policy
gradients or um or you know
distributional RL or or um
yeah any any of the hundreds of
algorithms that people have come up with
by hand. We instead of those things used
a neural network and the neural network
was basically able to output um any kind
of update rule it wanted in terms of
kind of targets that the agent would
move its its um the network towards.
Um and then what we did was we basically
learned how to adjust that meta network
such that when it was trained on each of
these environments it did as well as
possible. So this was literally like a
reinforcement learning process uh where
the agent is learning um which rule is
effective and basically just optimizing
that whole thing um through uh what we
call metagradient descent. So it's like
taking you know each of these things is
like there's some gradient-based rule
that's used here and then we look at we
kind of unroll the steps of that rule
over many steps and we take the gradient

leads to the best performance in
reinforcement learning.
